## Résumé de la structure du projet Swift

J'ai identifié l'architecture complète de gestion de la menubar, du popover et des métriques :

### **1. Affichage dans la MenuBar (Barre d'état)**

**Fichier principal :**
- `/Users/fcamblor/.archon/worktrees/third-party-oss/mac-ai-trackers/archon/task-feat-claude-model-weekly-metrics/AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/App/AIUsagesTrackersApp.swift`

**Lignes clés :**
- Ligne 55-68 : Structure `MenuBarExtra` avec `label: Text(usageStore.menuBarText)` qui affiche le texte formaté
- Ligne 66 : Le texte dynamique vient de `usageStore.menuBarText` (observable)
- Ligne 68 : Style `.menuBarExtraStyle(.window)` pour afficher comme une fenêtre popover

### **2. Affichage du Popover (Fenêtre au clic)**

**Fichier principal :**
- `/Users/fcamblor/.archon/worktrees/third-party-oss/mac-ai-trackers/archon/task-feat-claude-model-weekly-metrics/AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/App/Views/UsageDetailsView.swift`

**Lignes clés :**
- Ligne 4-5 : Commentaire expliquant la structure "Scrollable popover anchored to the menu bar item"
- Lignes 10-11 : Dimensions : `maxPopoverHeight = 480`, `popoverWidth = 320`
- Lignes 13-40 : Corps du popover avec ScrollView contenant des `AccountCardView`
- Lignes 58-72 : Footer avec le bouton Quit

### **3. Métriques affichées**

**Noms des métriques trouvées dans les tests :**
- `"session"` : durée de session (windowDuration: 300 minutes)
- `"weekly"` : limite hebdomadaire (windowDuration: 10080 minutes = 7 jours)

**Fichier de modèles :**
- `/Users/fcamblor/.archon/worktrees/third-party-oss/mac-ai-trackers/archon/task-feat-claude-model-weekly-metrics/AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Models/UsageModels.swift`

**Lignes clés (Enum UsageMetric) :**
- Ligne 57 : `.timeWindow(name: String, resetAt: ISODate, windowDuration: DurationMinutes, usagePercent: UsagePercent)` pour les métriques temporelles
- Ligne 58 : `.payAsYouGo(name: String, currentAmount: Double, currency: String)` pour les métriques de consommation

### **4. Affichage des métriques dans le popover**

**Fichier AccountCardView :**
- `/Users/fcamblor/.archon/worktrees/third-party-oss/mac-ai-trackers/archon/task-feat-claude-model-weekly-metrics/AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/App/Views/AccountCardView.swift`

**Lignes clés :**
- Lignes 33-35 : Boucle sur les métriques pour afficher chacune avec `metricRow(for: metric)`
- Lignes 51-69 : Switch polymorphe sur le type de métrique

**Fichiers de rendu des métriques :**
- **TimeWindowMetricRow** : `/Users/fcamblor/.archon/worktrees/third-party-oss/mac-ai-trackers/archon/task-feat-claude-model-weekly-metrics/AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/App/Views/TimeWindowMetricRow.swift`
  - Ligne 21 : Affiche le nom de la métrique capitalisé
  - Lignes 25-26 : Affiche le pourcentage d'utilisation
  - Lignes 29-32 : Barre de progression avec marqueur théorique
  - Lignes 35-41 : Temps restant et date de réinitialisation

- **PayAsYouGoMetricRow** : `/Users/fcamblor/.archon/worktrees/third-party-oss/mac-ai-trackers/archon/task-feat-claude-model-weekly-metrics/AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/App/Views/PayAsYouGoMetricRow.swift`
  - Lignes 9-17 : Formatage du montant avec currency code
  - Lignes 20-28 : Affichage du nom et du montant formaté

### **5. Formatage et calculs des métriques**

**Fichier principal :**
- `/Users/fcamblor/.archon/worktrees/third-party-oss/mac-ai-trackers/archon/task-feat-claude-model-weekly-metrics/AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Helpers/UsageComputations.swift`

**Fonctions clés :**
- Lignes 11-25 : `formatRemainingTime()` - formate le temps restant (ex: "2d 5h 13m")
- Lignes 47-61 : `formatResetDate()` - formate la date de réinitialisation (ex: "@ HH:mm", "Tomorrow @ HH:mm")
- Lignes 82-92 : Extension `sortedForDisplay()` sur Array pour trier les entrées
- Lignes 95-101 : Extension `vendorsWithMultipleAccounts()` pour identifier les vendeurs avec plusieurs comptes

**Fichier UsageStore :**
- `/Users/fcamblor/.archon/worktrees/third-party-oss/mac-ai-trackers/archon/task-feat-claude-model-weekly-metrics/AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Store/UsageStore.swift`

**Lignes clés :**
- Lignes 121-145 : Méthode `format(file:)` qui crée le texte de la menubar en abrégeant les noms des métriques (ligne 141: `abbreviation = name.prefix(1).uppercased()`)
- Exemple : "S 48% 2h 13m | W 7% 6d 6h 13m" (S=Session, W=Weekly)

### **6. Composants UI supplémentaires**

**GaugeBar** : `/Users/fcamblor/.archon/worktrees/third-party-oss/mac-ai-trackers/archon/task-feat-claude-model-weekly-metrics/AIUsagesTrackers/Sources/App/Views/GaugeBar.swift`
- Barre de progression horizontale avec couleurs codées (rouge si ≥90%, orange si ≥75%, sinon accent)
- Marqueur théorique montrant la consommation attendue

99	        }

103	        var container = encoder.container(keyedBy: CodingKeys.self)
104	        switch self {
105	        case let .timeWindow(name, resetAt, windowDuration, usagePercent):
106	            try container.encode(MetricKind.timeWindow, forKey: .type)
107	            try container.encode(name, forKey: .name)
108	            try container.encode(resetAt, forKey: .resetAt)
109	            try container.encode(windowDuration, forKey: .windowDuration)
110	            try container.encode(usagePercent, forKey: .usagePercent)
111	        case let .payAsYouGo(name, currentAmount, currency):
112	            try container.encode(MetricKind.payAsYouGo, forKey: .type)
113	            try container.encode(name, forKey: .name)
114	            try container.encode(currentAmount, forKey: .currentAmount)
115	            try container.encode(currency, forKey: .currency)
116	        case let .unknown(t):
117	            // Only the type discriminator round-trips; unknown fields are not preserved
118	            try container.encode(MetricKind.unknown(t), forKey: .type)
119	        }

7	/// A mock FileWatching that lets tests push arbitrary Data payloads.
8	/// Implemented as a class so `changes()` can hand out a fresh AsyncStream on each
9	/// call — required for start/stop/start lifecycle tests where the previous iteration
10	/// is cancelled before a new watchTask begins consuming again.
11	final class MockFileWatcher: FileWatching, @unchecked Sendable {
12	    // Protected by @MainActor in tests; @unchecked Sendable because AsyncStream.Continuation
13	    // is Sendable and all test access is single-threaded on the main actor.
14	    private var continuation: AsyncStream<Data>.Continuation?
15	    /// Buffers sends that arrive before the watch task calls changes(), or after a
16	    /// cancelled previous cycle. Flushed immediately when changes() is called.
17	    private var pendingData: [Data] = []
18	
19	    func changes() -> AsyncStream<Data> {
20	        // Finish the previous stream (if any) before creating a new one.
21	        // This prevents a stale task from consuming the new continuation's yields.
22	        continuation?.finish()
23	        var cont: AsyncStream<Data>.Continuation!
24	        let stream = AsyncStream<Data> { cont = $0 }
25	        continuation = cont
26	        // Flush sends that arrived before this call (e.g. test called send() before
27	        // the watch task had a chance to call changes()).
28	        for data in pendingData { cont.yield(data) }
29	        pendingData.removeAll()
30	        return stream
31	    }
32	
33	    func send(_ data: Data) {
34	        guard let cont = continuation else {
35	            pendingData.append(data)
36	            return
37	        }
38	        // .terminated means the previous cycle's consuming task was cancelled;
39	        // buffer the data so the next changes() call can deliver it.
40	        if case .terminated = cont.yield(data) {
41	            continuation = nil
42	            pendingData.append(data)
43	        }
44	    }
45	
46	    func finish() { continuation?.finish() }
47	}

49	/// A clock fixed at a given date, for deterministic remaining-time formatting.
50	struct FixedClock: ClockProvider {
51	    let date: Date
52	    init(_ date: Date) { self.date = date }
53	    func now() -> Date { date }
54	}

56	/// A clock whose date can be advanced between test steps to simulate time passing.
57	/// nonisolated(unsafe) — tests drive date changes from @MainActor, single-threaded; no concurrent writes.
58	@MainActor
59	final class MutableClock: ClockProvider {
60	    nonisolated(unsafe) var date: Date
61	    init(_ date: Date) { self.date = date }
62	    nonisolated func now() -> Date { date }
63	}

67	private struct EventuallyTimeoutError: Error {}
68	
69	/// Polls `condition` on the main actor at `interval` until it returns true or `timeout` expires.
70	@MainActor
71	private func eventually(
72	    timeout: TimeInterval = 2.0,
73	    interval: TimeInterval = 0.01,
74	    _ condition: @MainActor () -> Bool
75	) async throws {
76	    let deadline = Date(timeIntervalSinceNow: timeout)
77	    while true {
78	        if condition() { return }
79	        guard Date() < deadline else { throw EventuallyTimeoutError() }
80	        try await Task.sleep(nanoseconds: UInt64(interval * 1_000_000_000))
81	    }
82	}

1	# Swift testability and test coverage
2	
3	## Design for dependency injection from the start
4	
5	Every external dependency (network, file system, keychain, system clock, process execution) must be injectable via a protocol. This is non-negotiable for testability.
6	
7	```swift
8	// BAD — hard-coded dependency, untestable
9	actor ClaudeConnector {
10	    func fetch() async throws -> Data {
11	        let (data, _) = try await URLSession.shared.data(from: url)
12	        return data
13	    }
14	}
15	
16	// GOOD — injectable
17	protocol HTTPClient: Sendable {
18	    func data(from url: URL) async throws -> (Data, URLResponse)
19	}
20	
21	extension URLSession: HTTPClient {}
22	
23	actor ClaudeConnector {
24	    private let httpClient: HTTPClient
25	    init(httpClient: HTTPClient = URLSession.shared) {
26	        self.httpClient = httpClient
27	    }
28	}
29	```
30	
31	Apply this to: `URLSession`, `Keychain` access, `FileManager`, `Process` execution, `Date()` / clock.
32	
33	## Reuse expensive objects — do not instantiate per call
34	
35	`ISO8601DateFormatter`, `JSONDecoder`, `JSONEncoder`, and similar heavyweight objects must be created once and reused. Do not instantiate them inside functions called repeatedly.
36	
37	## Test every public method's primary path
38	
39	When creating a new type, every public method must have at least one test covering the success path. In particular:
40	- Network-fetching methods (`fetchUsages`, `fetchToken`, etc.)
41	- File I/O methods (write, read, rotate, merge)
42	- Lifecycle methods (start/stop idempotence)
43	- Error paths for external failures (network down, disk full, lock contention)
44	
45	## Force-unwrap only with compile-time-provable safety
46	
47	Never force-unwrap (`!`) on runtime values. For URL literals that are known-valid at compile time, use a clearly documented pattern:
48	
49	```swift
50	// Acceptable — string literal, provably valid
51	private static let apiBase = URL(string: "https://api.example.com")! // known-valid literal
52	
53	// BAD — runtime input, may fail
54	let url = URL(string: userInput)! // crash on bad input
55	```
56	
57	Even for literals, prefer a static `let` over inline force-unwrap to centralize the assumption.
58	
59	## Comment WHY, not WHAT
60	
61	Do not write comments that restate what the code does. Comments must explain *why* a non-obvious decision was made. If the code is self-explanatory, no comment is needed.
62	
63	```swift
64	// BAD
65	// Create the directory
66	try FileManager.default.createDirectory(at: dir, withIntermediateDirectories: true)
67	
68	// GOOD — no comment needed, the code is self-explanatory
69	
70	// GOOD — explains a non-obvious choice
71	// Using LOCK_NB to avoid blocking the cooperative thread pool; retry with backoff instead.
72	while flock(fd, LOCK_EX | LOCK_NB) != 0 { ... }
73	```
74	
75	## Name magic numbers
76	
77	Every numeric literal (other than 0, 1, true, false) must be a named constant explaining its purpose.
78	
79	```swift
80	// BAD
81	if fileSize > 5_242_880 { rotate() }
82	
83	// GOOD
84	private static let maxLogFileSizeBytes = 5_242_880 // 5 MB
85	if fileSize > Self.maxLogFileSizeBytes { rotate() }
86	```

# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.
